metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the vague 'docs link/section check passes' oracle in AC1 with a concrete proving command — e.g., a `grep -E` one-liner or a named CI script that asserts the presence of each required heading in `docs/mcp-auth.md`.
evidence: 'observable oracle: the file exists and a docs link/section check passes' — 'a docs link/section check' is undefined; no script name, no grep pattern, no CI job name cited.
The plan names `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` for manifest drift but names no analogous script for doc-section coverage.
findings.finding: AC1's proving command for content coverage is vague — 'a docs link/section check passes' is not a concrete, executable command; an executor cannot confirm which script/grep to run to verify all required subsections are present.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 ('observable oracle: the file exists and a docs link/section check passes')
scenarios: An agent marks AC1 complete after creating the file with minimal content, because the section-check oracle is undefined and unenforceable.
CI passes because no heading-check script exists, leaving required sections (e.g., residual risks, three client paths) absent from the shipped doc.
findings.suggested_fix: Example oracle addition: `grep -qE '^## (Security model|Behind-a-proxy|Client connection paths|Residual risks)' docs/mcp-auth.md` — or reference a named script (`scripts/check_doc_sections.py docs/mcp-auth.md`) that exits non-zero if any required heading is absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit step to the plan (and AC) to link `docs/mcp-auth.md` from `docs/README.md` under the Operator section, so the docs-index completeness gate (`scripts/check_docs_index.py --check`) passes as required by AC4.
evidence: scripts/check_docs_index.py find_unindexed(): every top-level docs/*.md not linked from docs/README.md is reported as a failure
.github/workflows/test.yml:276: `python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check`
docs/README.md shows all current docs/*.md files are individually linked under their audience sections; mcp-auth.md is absent
ABSENCE: neither the plan body nor any AC mentions adding mcp-auth.md to docs/README.md under the Operator section
findings.finding: Creating `docs/mcp-auth.md` without also updating `docs/README.md` to link it will cause the live docs-index completeness gate (scripts/check_docs_index.py, wired into test.yml:276 and gerrit-verify.yaml:235) to fail with 'unindexed living doc', blocking the `make test` pass required by AC4.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (`make lint && make test` pass); also the 'What' section describing `docs/mcp-auth.md`
scenarios: Implementer creates docs/mcp-auth.md as specified, runs `make test`, and the docs-index gate fails because mcp-auth.md is not referenced in docs/README.md — a CI failure that the plan gives no indication of.
A future contributor running the gate on the combined branch (the S7 'final consistency check') also hits this failure.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, append: 'Update `docs/README.md`'s Operator section to add an entry for `mcp-auth.md` (the docs-index completeness gate in CI will fail the build if the file is not linked from the index).' In AC1, append: '… and `docs/README.md` links the new file under the Operator section so the docs-index gate passes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and correct the 'CIMD' abbreviation in the interactive-client connection paths section of `docs/mcp-auth.md`: if the intent is CIBA (RFC 9156), confirm that Keycloak/Auth0/Okta support CIBA-based MCP client flows and use that term; if the intent is standard DCR (RFC 7591) only, remove the secondary acronym.
evidence: Plan text: '(2) automatic OAuth when the org IdP supports DCR or CIMD (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta can)'
CIMD does not appear in any RFC or OIDC spec; it is not in the repo anywhere
The closest known acronym is CIBA (Client-Initiated Backchannel Authentication, RFC 9156) or PKCE (RFC 7636), neither of which is what the operator guide likely intends
findings.finding: The plan asserts that 'automatic OAuth when the org IdP supports DCR or CIMD (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta can)' is one of the three interactive-client connection paths. 'CIMD' is not a recognized OAuth/OIDC specification acronym — it does not appear in RFC 6749, RFC 7591 (DCR), RFC 9156 (CIBA), or the MCP 2025-06-18 specification — making this an unverified capability assertion that operators trying to follow the guide will find unactionable.
findings.location: What section — docs/mcp-auth.md spec bullet: 'automatic OAuth when the org IdP supports DCR or CIMD (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta can)'
scenarios: An operator reads the guide, looks up 'CIMD' to configure their Keycloak instance, and finds no documentation — the connection path is unusable as written.
A security reviewer flags 'CIMD' as undefined and treats the interactive-client-paths section as unreliable, weakening confidence in the whole guide.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand and define the acronym 'CIMD' in the plan (e.g., spell out what it stands for and cite the relevant spec/RFC), or replace it with the correct standard term.
evidence: Plan text: 'automatic OAuth when the org IdP supports DCR or CIMD (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta can)'
Search for 'CIMD' across all docs/ returns no results
Search across the full repo returns no results
'DCR' (Dynamic Client Registration) is a known OAuth 2.0 RFC term, but CIMD is not a standard acronym in OAuth/OIDC RFCs
findings.finding: The acronym 'CIMD' is used in the client-connection-paths documentation requirement without being expanded or defined anywhere in the plan, the existing docs, or the codebase.
findings.location: What section — '(2) automatic OAuth when the org IdP supports DCR or CIMD (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta can)'
scenarios: Author writing docs/mcp-auth.md cannot accurately describe 'CIMD' without knowing what it stands for, risking an incorrect or fabricated expansion.
Third-party operators reading the guide cannot act on a protocol acronym they cannot identify or look up.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete cardinality/enumeration check to AC3 — e.g., a grep command or script invocation that asserts each S1–S6 key name appears in `docs/mcp-reference.md` and `docs/env-vars.md`, not just that the drift gate is green.
evidence: 'all new keys present' is a cardinality claim (taxonomy rule 5 — missing cardinality assertion).

`make test` proves drift-gate consistency between code and generated docs, but does NOT independently assert a specific count or enumeration of keys; a partially regenerated set could still pass if the drift gate only checks the regenerated slice.
findings.finding: AC3 asserts 'all new keys present' in the regenerated reference files but supplies no cardinality-asserting command; `make test` passing is necessary but not sufficient to prove every key from S1–S6 is actually included (a subset could be regenerated and CI could still be green).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('observable oracle: `make test` / the manifest-check job is green with all new keys present')
scenarios: S6's keys are not included in the final regeneration; `make test` is green because the drift gate compares only what was regenerated; the missing keys are never caught in S7.
findings.suggested_fix: Add alongside `make test`: `grep -qF 'AUTH_STATIC_TOKEN\|AUTH_JWT_SECRET\|...' docs/env-vars.md` (list the canonical key names from S1–S6) — or reference an existing key-enumeration fixture that the drift gate compares against.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the target ADR filename (e.g., `docs/adr/0050-mcp-auth.md`) explicitly in the plan to avoid numbering collisions with concurrent stories.
evidence: docs/adr/ contains 0001 through 0049 with strict sequential numbering
Plan text: 'A new ADR under docs/adr/ recording…' — no filename specified
ABSENCE: no proposed filename or number anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the ADR filename or sequential number for the new ADR under docs/adr/. The existing convention is strictly sequential (0001 through 0049); the next available number is 0050. Without specifying the filename, the executor must choose arbitrarily, risking a collision if another story in the epic also creates an ADR concurrently.
findings.location: What section / 'A new ADR under docs/adr/' bullet
scenarios: A concurrent story (or a second executor pass) also creates an ADR and picks 0050, causing a filename collision on the combined branch.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'A new ADR under docs/adr/' to 'A new ADR at docs/adr/0050-mcp-auth.md' (verify 0050 is available at merge time).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `required_scopes` is explicitly introduced by one of S1–S6 (with a concrete env var / config key name) before S7 documents it as a gate alongside `REBAR_MCP_READONLY`/`ALLOW_LLM`/`ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`; if it is a new S1–S6 deliverable, name the story that owns it and confirm the key name matches what will appear in `MCP_ENV_VARS`.
evidence: Search for 'required_scopes' in entire repo returns zero matches.
src/rebar/mcp_server.py MCP_ENV_VARS tuple contains only REBAR_ROOT, REBAR_MCP_READONLY, REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM, REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC — no required_scopes.
server.json contains only those same four env vars.
Plan phrase: 'gated only by server-level `required_scopes` and the existing `REBAR_MCP_READONLY`/`ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`/`ALLOW_LLM` env gates, which now also gate remote actors'
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'server-level `required_scopes`' as an existing gate alongside the pre-existing REBAR_MCP_READONLY/ALLOW_LLM/ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC env gates, but `required_scopes` does not appear anywhere in the codebase (`src/`, `server.json`, `MCP_ENV_VARS`). If `required_scopes` is a new key introduced by S1–S6 (not yet merged), the plan's wording should make that dependency explicit rather than listing it as a co-equal existing gate; if it is expected to already exist, it is absent.
findings.location: What section — residual risks paragraph: 'gated only by server-level `required_scopes` and the existing `REBAR_MCP_READONLY`/`ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`/`ALLOW_LLM` env gates'
scenarios: If required_scopes is supposed to be documented in docs/mcp-auth.md as an already-existing feature but was never implemented by S1–S6, the operator guide will document a config key that has no effect.

If the docs section on residual risks says auth is gated by required_scopes but that key does not exist at runtime, operators following the guide will misconfigure their deployments believing scope enforcement is active.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit cross-reference: 'required_scopes is introduced by S[N] as config key `auth_required_scopes` / env `REBAR_MCP_REQUIRED_SCOPES`' so S7's documentation task has a concrete, verifiable target rather than an unanchored name.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add updating `docs/README.md` (Operator section) with a link to the new `docs/mcp-auth.md` to the plan's scope/AC.
evidence: docs/README.md Operator section (lines 77–100) lists operator docs with explicit links — mcp-auth.md is absent
ABSENCE: docs/README.md not mentioned anywhere in the S7 plan
findings.finding: The plan creates docs/mcp-auth.md (a new operator guide) but does not mention updating docs/README.md, which has an Operator section that links every operator doc. A new operator guide file left unlinked from docs/README.md is an unenumerated consumer; the Operator section currently lists config.md, env-vars.md, jira-sync-setup.md, gerrit-aws-setup.md, etc., but has no entry for mcp-auth.md.
findings.location: What section / 'New docs/mcp-auth.md' bullet; docs/README.md Operator section
scenarios: An operator looking for the MCP auth guide via docs/README.md cannot find it because no link is added; the guide exists as an orphaned file with no discoverability path from the docs index.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope or AC1: 'docs/README.md Operator section gets a link entry for docs/mcp-auth.md.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify or replace the four security identifiers cited in the ADR (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, PVE-2026-93393): either confirm each resolves in NVD/GHSA/OSV and link the URL, or replace with the actual verified identifier, or (for the DNS-rebinding and confused-deputy patterns) cite the generic vulnerability class with a reference to the existing codebase defense (e.g., `editor_server.py` DNS-rebinding guard) rather than an unverifiable CVE number.
evidence: Plan text: 'the transport hardening (citing GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f DNS-rebinding, oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 forwarded-header, CVE-2026-27124 confused-deputy)'
Plan text: 'the composite audience choke point (motivated by the SDK having no `.resource` backstop + the audience-confusion precedent PVE-2026-93393)'
Search for all four identifiers in the repo returns zero results
CVE-2026-* identifiers are future-dated and have no NVD entries verifiable at review time; PVE-* is not a recognized advisory namespace (it is not NVD, GitHub Security Advisories, OSV, or PyPA)
findings.finding: The ADR section cites four specific security identifiers — GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, and PVE-2026-93393 — as motivating the transport-hardening decisions, but none of these identifiers can be verified: CVE-2026-* numbers are future-dated (no NVD/MITRE entries exist at review time), and PVE-2026-93393 does not match any known advisory namespace. An ADR intended for 'rigorous security review' that cites phantom identifiers is worse than citing no identifiers — it actively undermines the document's credibility.
findings.location: What section — ADR bullet: 'the transport hardening (citing GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f DNS-rebinding, oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 forwarded-header, CVE-2026-27124 confused-deputy); and the explicitly deferred items'
scenarios: A security auditor attempts to look up GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f or CVE-2026-40575 and finds no record — the ADR's entire transport-hardening rationale collapses.
A future maintainer cites this ADR in a compliance review; the phantom CVEs are flagged as fabricated, casting doubt on all other ADR claims.
The 'audience-confusion precedent PVE-2026-93393' motivates the composite-audience design; if the identifier is invalid, the design rationale becomes ungrounded.
findings.suggested_fix: For GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f: check https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f. For CVE-2026-*: these cannot be real CVEs published before 2026; if the intent is to cite a future/hypothetical advisory, remove the CVE framing and describe the vulnerability class directly (e.g., 'DNS-rebinding via unvalidated Host header, mitigated by `http_allowed_hosts`'). For PVE-2026-93393: this namespace does not match PyPA (PYSEC-*), NVD, or GHSA — either find the real identifier or describe the audience-confusion attack class (token issued for resource A accepted by resource B) without a phantom number.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify and ground the 'build-own vs standalone `fastmcp`' ADR comparison: explicitly name the standalone package (`jlowin/fastmcp` on PyPI), distinguish it from `mcp.server.fastmcp` (the SDK module already in use), and either cite a verifiable advisory/changelog entry for the 'CVE history' claim or replace it with a specific, verifiable description of the auth-stability gap (e.g., 'auth middleware was added post-1.0 without a stability commitment in jlowin/fastmcp ≤X.Y').
evidence: Plan text: 'building our own verifiers on the official SDK seam vs adopting standalone `fastmcp` (semver-exempt auth, CVE history)'
Codebase: `from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP` in src/rebar/mcp_server.py and scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py — this is the official MCP SDK, not the standalone package
resolve_symbol confirms mcp.server.fastmcp EXISTS in site-packages (official SDK)
No CVE or advisory for standalone fastmcp is cited or verifiable in the repo
findings.finding: The plan's ADR justifies the 'build-own verifiers' decision partly by characterizing standalone `fastmcp` as having 'semver-exempt auth, CVE history' — but the codebase already imports `mcp.server.fastmcp` (the official MCP Python SDK module, not the standalone `fastmcp` PyPI package by `jlowin`). The characterization may conflate two different packages, and the 'CVE history' claim against standalone fastmcp is unverified in any repo artifact, making this an unverified capability assertion that the ADR is supposed to document authoritatively.
findings.location: What section — ADR bullet: 'building our own verifiers on the official SDK seam vs adopting standalone `fastmcp` (semver-exempt auth, CVE history)'
scenarios: A maintainer reading the ADR cannot tell whether 'standalone fastmcp' refers to the jlowin/fastmcp package or the mcp.server.fastmcp module — the comparison is ambiguous and potentially misleading.
A security reviewer tries to look up the 'CVE history' of standalone fastmcp and finds no evidence, weakening the build-vs-adopt justification.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify each cited advisory ID (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, PVE-2026-93393, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124) against the public NVD/GHSA database before committing the ADR, and confirm the cited description ('DNS-rebinding', 'forwarded-header', 'confused-deputy', 'audience-confusion') matches the actual advisory.
evidence: Plan text: 'the transport hardening (citing GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f DNS-rebinding, oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 forwarded-header, CVE-2026-27124 confused-deputy)'
Plan text: 'the composite audience choke point (motivated by the SDK having no `.resource` backstop + the audience-confusion precedent PVE-2026-93393)'
ABSENCE: none of GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, PVE-2026-93393 appear anywhere in the repo
Evidence class: Unverified — cannot probe NVD/GHSA in this environment; all four are 2026-dated and may predate public disclosure or be incorrectly recalled
Impact: ADR is a security audit artifact; incorrect CVE citations undermine its trustworthiness with third-party reviewers
findings.finding: The ADR is required to cite four specific security advisory identifiers (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, PVE-2026-93393, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124) as motivation for transport-hardening decisions, but none of these identifiers exist in the repo and none can be verified as real published advisories in this environment. If any are fabricated or mis-recalled, the ADR will misinform security reviewers who rely on it as an audit artifact.
findings.location: What section / ADR bullet: 'the transport hardening (citing GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f DNS-rebinding, oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 forwarded-header, CVE-2026-27124 confused-deputy); and the explicitly deferred items'
scenarios: Security auditor looks up GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f and finds it doesn't exist or refers to an unrelated issue — the ADR rationale collapses.
A CVE number is real but the description ('DNS-rebinding') doesn't match — the hardening decision appears to lack a legitimate cited basis.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact Claude.ai MCP connector config field name for custom request headers (searching the Claude.ai documentation or testing the MCP connection wizard) before publishing `docs/mcp-auth.md`, and confirm whether it is `static_headers`, `headers`, or another name.
evidence: Plan text: 'static bearer via Claude Code `--header "Authorization: Bearer …"` / Claude.ai `static_headers` — pairs with the static verifier'
ABSENCE: no matches for 'static_headers' anywhere in the codebase
Evidence class: Unverified — Claude.ai connector config is an external SaaS surface not probed in this environment
If the field is named differently (e.g., 'headers', 'custom_headers'), operators following the guide will fail silently to authenticate
findings.finding: The operator guide is required to document Claude.ai's `static_headers` config field as the mechanism for passing a static bearer token, but this is an external SaaS product's configuration surface. The field name `static_headers` is not grounded anywhere in the repo and cannot be verified against Claude.ai's actual connector config schema in this environment.
findings.location: What section / operator guide bullet: '(1) static bearer via Claude Code `--header "Authorization: Bearer …"` / Claude.ai `static_headers` — pairs with the static verifier'
scenarios: An operator copies the `static_headers` example from the guide into their Claude.ai MCP connector config, uses an incorrect field name, and the bearer token is never sent — authentication fails with no clear error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the ADR's target filename and number (e.g., `docs/adr/0044-mcp-auth.md`) or cite the project's ADR numbering convention so the executor has a concrete default.
evidence: 'A new ADR under `docs/adr/`' — no filename, no number, no naming convention stated.
ABSENCE: no reference to an existing ADR index or numbering scheme in the plan text.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies the ADR filename/numbering convention (e.g., `docs/adr/NNNN-mcp-auth.md`), leaving the executor to choose arbitrarily; this is a defaultable gap that should be resolved in the plan.
findings.location: What section — ADR bullet; Scope section
scenarios: Executor picks a number that collides with an existing ADR, or uses an inconsistent naming pattern, requiring a rename after review.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'Create `docs/adr/NNNN-mcp-auth.md` where NNNN is the next sequential ADR number per `docs/adr/README.md` (or explicitly `0044` if that is the next available).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the S7 consistency-check scope to explicitly audit (and update if needed) `README.md`, `CLAUDE.md`, and any other human-authored docs that reference server startup, HTTP transport, or auth configuration — or explicitly state they are out-of-scope with justification.
evidence: Plan lists only '`docs/mcp-reference.md` and `docs/env-vars.md` are fully regenerated … and the CI drift gates for them and for `server.json` pass'
No mention of auditing README.md, CLAUDE.md, or other human-authored narrative docs for stale references to transport/auth setup
S1–S6 introduce significant new env vars, config keys, and an HTTP transport mode that operators configure — common locations for startup instructions
findings.finding: The plan's final consistency check explicitly covers only `docs/mcp-reference.md`, `docs/env-vars.md`, and `server.json` via drift gates, but does not mention auditing or updating human-authored docs (e.g., `README.md`, `CLAUDE.md`) that likely reference server startup, transport configuration, or environment variables and may become stale once S1–S6 introduce the new HTTP+auth surface.
findings.location: ## What — 'Final reference/manifest consistency check' bullet; AC line 3
scenarios: An operator reads README.md quick-start and finds no mention of the new HTTP transport or auth env vars, or finds outdated instructions that conflict with the new auth requirements
A contributor reads CLAUDE.md and follows a stale 'how to run' section that omits required auth configuration for the HTTP transport
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet to the 'Final reference/manifest consistency check' section: 'Grep `README.md` and `CLAUDE.md` for HTTP-transport and auth-related content; update quick-start examples and env-var lists to reflect the new auth surface, or explicitly note they defer to `docs/mcp-auth.md` with a link.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an explicit ownership/sequencing rule for the shared generated files (`docs/mcp-reference.md`, `docs/env-vars.md`): either mandate strict sequential integration of S1–S6 before any regeneration, or document the merge/rebase strategy (e.g., 'always re-run the full regeneration script on the combined branch — never accept a merge of partially-regenerated files') and add a remediation note to the AC.
evidence: 'S1–S6 each regenerated their slice; S7 verifies the union'
Plan does not specify whether S1–S6 are strictly sequential or can be developed in parallel branches
No merge-conflict resolution strategy or ownership rule stated for the shared generated files
ABSENCE: no 'if regeneration detects a conflict, do X' recovery step
findings.finding: The plan says S1–S6 each 'regenerates its slice' of the shared reference files (`docs/mcp-reference.md`, `docs/env-vars.md`), but does not specify the ownership/merge strategy when those stories are developed on parallel feature branches. Each branch's regeneration of the shared files will produce git merge conflicts or silent overwrites, leaving the shared-doc state in a partially-correct intermediate condition until S7 verifies the union — but S7 has no defined remediation path if the merge is already corrupt.
findings.location: ## What — Final reference/manifest consistency check paragraph; ## Scope; AC bullet 3
scenarios: Two stories (e.g. S3 and S5) are developed on parallel branches, each running the regeneration script against their own branch; when merged, the generated file contains one story's additions but not the other's, and the CI gate on S7's combined branch catches this only after a potentially confusing merge conflict or silent overwrite.
A developer merges S4 into S6's branch before S7; the regenerated env-vars.md reflects only S4+S6 keys; S1–S3/S5 keys are missing; S7's CI gate fails with no clear remediation path documented.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'The reference-doc regeneration scripts are authoritative and must be re-run on the fully-merged combined branch (not cherry-picked across branch merges). Each story's PR should merge sequentially into a shared integration branch; S7 re-runs the full regeneration as its first step, treating any prior per-story regeneration as non-authoritative. If CI detects drift, the fix is always to re-run the generation script, never to hand-edit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add regeneration of `tests/golden/config_surface.json` (via `python scripts/gen_config_surface.py` or equivalent) to AC3 and the final consistency check, alongside the three already-named drift gates.
evidence: AC3 text: 'docs/mcp-reference.md and docs/env-vars.md are fully regenerated … and the CI drift gates for them and for server.json pass'

tests/golden/config_surface.json exists with a comment: 'CI anti-regression guard for the config surface … Removing a key without a tombstone fails tests/unit/test_config_surface_golden.py. Regenerate deliberately when you intentionally add/retire a surface.'
tests/golden/config_surface.json currently lists only mcp.allow_jira_sync, mcp.allow_llm, mcp.readonly — no mcp.auth_* keys
S6 ticket body confirms auth_custom_import is added to _SECTIONS['mcp'] / McpConfig
ABSENCE: tests/golden/config_surface.json not mentioned anywhere in S7 plan
findings.finding: The plan's final consistency check (AC3) names only three drift gates (docs/mcp-reference.md, docs/env-vars.md, server.json via check_server_manifest.py) but omits tests/golden/config_surface.json, which is a fourth CI-enforced drift gate (tests/unit/test_config_surface_golden.py) that pins every config key and canonical env-var name. S1–S6 add multiple new mcp.auth_* config keys and REBAR_MCP_AUTH_* env-var names to _SECTIONS['mcp']; the golden file currently has no mcp.auth_* entries. If S7 does not regenerate this golden, test_config_surface_golden.py will fail CI on the combined branch.
findings.location: AC3 / 'Final reference/manifest consistency check' section
scenarios: S7 executor runs make test on the combined branch after S1–S6; test_config_surface_golden.py fails because mcp.auth_* keys are present in _SECTIONS but absent from the golden, causing a CI red that blocks the story from closing.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, add: 'and `tests/golden/config_surface.json` is regenerated to include all new `mcp.auth_*` config keys and `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_*` env-var names added by S1–S6 (observable oracle: `test_config_surface_golden.py` is green on the combined branch).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `make typecheck` to S7 AC4 (e.g., '`make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass') so the terminal integration story fulfils parent epic AC8's requirement that all three gates pass on the final integrated branch.
evidence: S7 AC4: '`make lint && make test` pass (docs/mechanical leaf; no typecheck-relevant code added here)'
Parent epic AC8: '`make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass on the final integrated branch.'
S7 Why/Scope: 'S7 confirms the integrated whole is consistent' — S7 is the designated integration/verification story.
S1–S6 each include typecheck in their own per-story ACs, but none are positioned to verify the combined branch state that S7 is supposed to confirm.
findings.finding: S7 AC4 omits `make typecheck` (stating 'docs/mechanical leaf; no typecheck-relevant code added here'), but the parent epic AC8 requires `make lint && make typecheck && make test` to pass 'on the final integrated branch' — and S7 is the terminal story explicitly responsible for confirming the integrated S1–S6+S7 branch is consistent. No sibling story is positioned to assert typecheck passes on the combined branch.
findings.location: S7 Acceptance Criteria, AC4 vs parent epic (3393-83ca-5501-4e6e) Acceptance Criteria AC8
scenarios: A type error introduced by a sibling story's code (S1–S6) is never caught on the combined branch because S7 — the only story verifying the integrated state — does not require typecheck to pass.
The epic closes with all child stories green, but `make typecheck` silently fails on the integrated branch, violating parent AC8.
findings.suggested_fix: Change S7 AC4 to: '`make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass on the integrated branch (S7 adds no new type-annotated code, so typecheck should pass trivially; this AC confirms the combined S1–S6+S7 state satisfies the parent epic's integration gate).' This aligns with the parent's AC8 without contradicting S7's 'docs/mechanical leaf' characterization.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `docs/mcp-auth.md`'s static-verifier flat-config example explicitly warns operators to supply the token via environment-variable substitution (or a secrets manager) rather than as a literal value in a committed config file, and notes that config files containing literal tokens must be excluded from VCS.
evidence: Plan specifies: 'each of the five verifier modes (static, jwt, introspection, proxy, custom) with a flat-config example each'
Plan specifies: 'static-token entropy … document the generator one-liner'
Plan says 'tokens/secrets never logged' but does not say the guide warns against committing the literal token to VCS or recommends env-var substitution for the config value
ABSENCE: no mention of 'env var injection', 'VCS exclusion', '.gitignore', 'file permissions', or 'secrets manager' in the context of the static verifier flat-config example
findings.finding: The operator guide's flat-config example for the `static` verifier mode will contain a literal bearer-token value, but the plan does not specify that the guide will advise operators against embedding the raw secret in a committed config file — it omits guidance on env-var injection, VCS exclusion, or file-permission hardening for config files containing the token.
findings.location: docs/mcp-auth.md spec — 'each of the five verifier modes with a flat-config example each' / 'residual risks … static-token entropy' / 'document the generator one-liner'
scenarios: An operator follows the flat-config example verbatim, placing a literal >=128-bit token in rebar's config file, commits that file to a (possibly private but shared) repo, and the static bearer secret is exposed to all repo readers.
A secrets-scanning tool (e.g. trufflehog, gitleaks) flags the committed token after it is rotated, revealing the former token in git history.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC for `docs/mcp-auth.md`: 'The static-verifier example MUST show the token value as an env-var reference (e.g. `token: "${REBAR_MCP_STATIC_TOKEN}"`) rather than a literal string, with an explicit note that literal secrets in config files must never be committed to VCS.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the exact, complete set of `auth_strategies` config key values (the closed vocabulary) explicitly in the plan — confirm whether the five mode names `static`, `jwt`, `introspection`, `proxy`, `custom` are the precise string values the config parser accepts, or if they differ.
evidence: Plan text: 'each of the five verifier modes (`static`, `jwt`, `introspection`, `proxy`, `custom`) with a flat-config example each, and the closed `auth_strategies` vocabulary'
The plan says 'closed' but never states the five prose-named modes ARE its exact enum values vs. some other spelling/naming
No `auth_strategies` definition found anywhere in docs/ or the codebase search results
An executor writing docs must know whether e.g. 'bearer', 'oidc', or other values exist
findings.finding: The plan mandates documenting 'the closed `auth_strategies` vocabulary' but never definitively enumerates the exact set of allowed values in the plan itself — the five mode names (static, jwt, introspection, proxy, custom) appear only in separate prose, and the plan never explicitly states these five ARE the complete `auth_strategies` enum.
findings.location: What section — 'the closed `auth_strategies` vocabulary'; AC1 — '`auth_strategies` vocabulary'
scenarios: Author of docs/mcp-auth.md writes the `auth_strategies` vocabulary table with a value set that doesn't match what S1-S6 actually shipped, causing the guide to be incorrect from day one.
A third-party operator reading the guide encounters an `auth_strategies` value in error messages that doesn't appear in the documented vocabulary.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the What section: 'The `auth_strategies` key accepts exactly five values: `static`, `jwt`, `introspection`, `proxy`, `custom` — matching the five verifier mode names; no other values are valid.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the config key name(s) and format for the `custom` verifier mode in S7's What section (e.g., 'the `custom` mode is configured via `auth_custom_import: "module:factory"` — a `module:factory` import string') so the author has a concrete schema to document.
evidence: Plan AC1: 'all five verifier modes with a flat-config example each'
Plan What section only says '(for multiple issuers, use the `custom` verifier)' — no config key or format given
The `custom` verifier is implemented in S6 via `auth_custom_import`, but S7 neither names this key nor specifies the format (e.g., `module:factory` string)
Prior plan-review cache entry for S6 confirms `auth_custom_import` as the key name, but S7 does not reference it
findings.finding: AC1 requires 'a flat-config example each' for all five verifier modes including `custom`, but S7 never specifies the config schema for `custom` mode — the key name(s) and format an operator would write to activate it are not defined in this plan.
findings.location: AC1 — 'all five verifier modes with a flat-config example each'; What section — 'for multiple issuers, use the `custom` verifier'
scenarios: Author of docs/mcp-auth.md writes a `custom` flat-config example with the wrong key name or format, producing a guide that operators cannot follow.
CI docs-drift gate passes (since the gate only checks key registration, not example correctness), leaving a silently wrong operator guide.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'The `custom` verifier flat-config example uses `auth_custom_import: "mypackage.auth:make_verifier"` (the `module:factory` import string format defined in S6).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
